provenance.source_corpus: Substrate comparison for short-term success of a multispecies tree plantation in thickened tailings of a boreal gold mine

source_spans.text: Trees did not survive the first winter when planted directly in tailings
Trees did not survive the first winter when planted directly in tailings
compost addition to tailings increased survival through substrate structure improvement
compost addition to tailings increased survival through substrate structure improvement
Compost added to tailings improved the structure of the mixture by increasing the macroporosity
Compost added to tailings improved the structure of the mixture by increasing the macroporosity and decreasing the bulk density
Adding compost to tailings or mineral soil improved the P nutrition of trees
trees grown in the thicker topsoil showed the highest N nutrition and aerial growth
trees grown in the thicker topsoil showed the highest N nutrition and aerial growth
trees grown in the thicker topsoil showed the highest N nutrition and aerial growth
substrate macroporosity appeared to be positively and linearly correlated to poplar above ground biomass
substrate macroporosity appeared to be positively and linearly correlated to poplar above ground biomass
nitrogen concentrations were positively and linearly correlated with all aboveground growth parameters
nitrogen concentrations were positively and linearly correlated with all aboveground growth parameters
Compost addition enriched the mixtures (tailings and mineral soil) in total Ca and P
trees grown in these treatments showed significantly improved P foliar concentrations
proximity of the neutral tailings under thin topsoil layers may have increased the topsoil pH
proximity of the neutral tailings under thin topsoil layers may have increased the topsoil pH

nodes.name: absorption of Cd, Cu, and Ni

source_spans.text: proximity of the neutral tailings under thin topsoil layers may have increased the topsoil pH... which could have participated in decreased absorption of Cd, Cu, and Ni
acidic pH of the topsoil may have increased the Zn availability to trees
